design.title: Design Problems
design.description: Architect data structures from scratch — implement stacks, queues, and complex systems using fundamental operations as building blocks.

implement-queue-using-stacks.title: Implement Queue using Stacks
implement-queue-using-stacks.description: Implement a first-in-first-out (FIFO) queue using only two stacks. The implemented queue should support push, pop, peek, and empty operations.

examples.explanation:
push(1)
push(2)
peek() -> 1
pop() -> 1
empty() -> false

constraints: At most 100 calls will be made to push, pop, peek, and empty.
All the calls to pop and peek are valid.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Transfer on Every Pop/Peek
approaches.intuition: Use one stack for push. Whenever pop or peek is called, transfer all elements to another stack to reverse order, perform operation, then transfer back.
pros: Easy to reason about reversal logic

approaches.title: Improved — Lazy Transfer (Two Stack Method)
approaches.intuition: Use two stacks: inStack for push operations, outStack for pop/peek operations. Only transfer elements when outStack is empty.
steps: If outStack is empty, transfer all from inStack to outStack.

approaches.title: Optimal — Lazy Transfer (Amortized O(1))
approaches.intuition: Each element is moved at most once from inStack to outStack. Therefore, although a single operation may take O(n), the amortized cost per operation is O(1).
steps: Pop/peek only transfers when outStack is empty.
Each element is transferred at most once.

implement-stack-using-queues.title: Implement Stack using Queues
implement-stack-using-queues.description: Implement a last-in-first-out (LIFO) stack using only two queues. The implemented stack should support push, pop, top, and empty operations.

examples.explanation:
push(1)
push(2)
top() -> 2
pop() -> 2
empty() -> false

constraints: At most 100 calls will be made to push, pop, top, and empty.
All calls to pop and top are valid.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Two Queues (Expensive Pop)
approaches.intuition: Push elements into q1. For pop, move all elements except last into q2, remove last, then swap queues.
steps: Move first n-1 elements from q1 to q2.

approaches.title: Improved — Two Queues (Expensive Push)
approaches.intuition: Maintain stack order inside q1 at all times. On push, insert into q2, move all from q1 to q2, then swap.
steps: Move all elements from q1 to q2.
Now q1 always has stack order (top at front).
pros: Pop and top are constant time

approaches.title: Optimal — One Queue Only
approaches.intuition: Use a single queue. After pushing an element, rotate the queue so that the new element comes to the front.
steps: Rotate previous elements to back (size-1 times).
Now the newest element is at front.

design-browser-history.title: Design Browser History
design-browser-history.description: You have a browser with one tab. You start on the homepage. Implement the BrowserHistory class that supports visit, back, and forward operations.

examples.explanation:
BrowserHistory("leetcode.com")
visit("google.com")
visit("facebook.com")
visit("youtube.com")
back(1) -> facebook.com
back(1) -> google.com
forward(1) -> facebook.com
visit("linkedin.com")
forward(2) -> linkedin.com
back(2) -> google.com
back(7) -> leetcode.com

constraints: homepage and url consist of '.' or lowercase English letters.
At most 5000 calls will be made to visit, back, and forward.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Two Stacks
approaches.intuition: Use two stacks: backStack and forwardStack. Visiting clears forwardStack. Back moves current to forwardStack. Forward moves current to backStack.
steps: Visit: push current to backStack, clear forwardStack.

approaches.title: Improved — Single Array with Pointer
approaches.intuition: Store all visited URLs in an array. Maintain a current index pointer. On visit, truncate forward history manually.
steps: Back/Forward: move pointer safely within bounds.

approaches.title: Optimal — Array with Logical End Pointer
approaches.intuition: Use array with current pointer and max pointer (logical end). Avoid slicing. Simply overwrite forward history logically.

design-hit-counter.title: Design Hit Counter
design-hit-counter.description: Design a hit counter that counts the number of hits received in the past 5 minutes (300 seconds). Each function accepts a timestamp (in seconds granularity). It is guaranteed that calls are made in chronological order.

examples.explanation:
At timestamp 4 → hits at 1,2,3 → total = 3
At timestamp 300 → hits at 1,2,3,300 → total = 4
At timestamp 301 → hits at 2,3,300 → total = 3

constraints: All calls are made in chronological order.
At most 300 calls will be made to hit and getHits.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Store All Timestamps
approaches.intuition: Store every hit timestamp in an array. For getHits, iterate through all timestamps and count those within the last 300 seconds.
steps: On getHits(timestamp): iterate and count hits >= timestamp - 299.
cons: Inefficient for large number of hits
Does not clean up old timestamps

approaches.title: Improved — Queue with Cleanup
approaches.intuition: Maintain a queue of timestamps. On each getHits or hit, remove timestamps older than 300 seconds.
steps: Use a queue to store timestamps.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w ≤ hits in 5 minutes
cons: Uses dynamic memory proportional to recent hits

approaches.title: Optimal — Circular Buffer with Counts
approaches.intuition: Since the window is fixed at 300 seconds, use two arrays of size 300: one for timestamps and one for hit counts. Use modulo 300 indexing to reuse slots.
steps: For getHits: sum counts where timestamp - times[i] < 300.

design-circular-queue.title: Design Circular Queue
design-circular-queue.description: Design your implementation of the circular queue. The circular queue is a linear data structure in which the operations are performed based on FIFO principle and the last position is connected back to the first position to make a circle.

examples.explanation:
MyCircularQueue(3)
enQueue(1)
enQueue(2)
enQueue(3)
enQueue(4) -> false (full)
Rear() -> 3
isFull() -> true
deQueue() -> true
enQueue(4) -> true
Rear() -> 4

constraints: At most 3000 calls will be made to enQueue, deQueue, Front, Rear, isEmpty, and isFull.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Dynamic Array with Shifting
approaches.intuition: Use a normal array. On deQueue, shift all elements left. This works but defeats circular efficiency.
steps: deQueue: remove first element using shift.

approaches.title: Optimal — Ring Buffer with Head/Tail Pointers
approaches.intuition: Use fixed-size array. Maintain head, tail, and count. Use modulo arithmetic for circular behavior.
steps: enQueue: write at tail, move tail = (tail+1)%k.

approaches.title: Alternative — Without Count (Sacrifice One Slot)
approaches.intuition: Instead of tracking count, use condition: queue is full when (tail + 1) % k === head. This sacrifices one slot to distinguish full from empty.
steps: Use head and tail pointers only.

design-twitter.title: Design Twitter
design-twitter.description: Design a simplified version of Twitter where users can post tweets, follow/unfollow other users, and retrieve the 10 most recent tweets in the user's news feed. The news feed must be ordered from most recent to least recent.

examples.explanation:
User 1 posts tweet 5
User 1 news feed -> [5]
User 1 follows user 2
User 2 posts tweet 6
User 1 news feed -> [6,5]
User 1 unfollows user 2
User 1 news feed -> [5]

constraints: At most 3 * 10^4 calls will be made.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Collect, Sort, Return
approaches.intuition: Store all tweets globally. For getNewsFeed, filter tweets from the user and their followees, sort by timestamp, and return top 10.
steps: Maintain global tweet list with timestamps.

approaches.title: Improved — Per-User Tweet Lists + Merge
approaches.intuition: Store tweets per user. For getNewsFeed, collect tweets from user and followees, merge them, then sort top 10.
steps: On getNewsFeed: gather all relevant tweets.

approaches.title: Optimal — Min-Heap K-Way Merge
approaches.intuition: Each user’s tweets are already sorted by time. Use a max heap (or min heap with negated time) to perform k-way merge and extract top 10 tweets efficiently.
steps: Push most recent tweet from each relevant user into heap.
Extract max, then push next tweet from same user.

max-frequency-stack.title: Maximum Frequency Stack
max-frequency-stack.description: Design a stack-like data structure that supports push and pop operations. The pop operation should remove and return the most frequent element in the stack. If there is a tie for most frequent, return the most recently pushed element among them.

examples.explanation:
push(5)
push(7)
push(5)
push(7)
push(4)
push(5)
pop() -> 5 (freq=3)
pop() -> 7 (freq=2, most recent among freq=2)
pop() -> 5
pop() -> 4

constraints: At most 2 * 10^4 calls will be made to push and pop.
It is guaranteed that pop will not be called on an empty stack.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Scan on Every Pop
approaches.intuition: Maintain a stack and a frequency map. On pop, scan the entire stack to find the most frequent element (breaking ties by recency).
steps: On pop: scan to find max frequency element.

approaches.title: Improved — Maintain Frequency Map Incrementally
approaches.intuition: Keep track of frequencies dynamically and track the current maximum frequency. Still scan during pop but avoid recomputing max frequency.
steps: On pop: scan from top to find element with maxFreq.

approaches.title: Optimal — Group-by-Frequency Stacks
approaches.intuition: Maintain two maps: val → frequency and frequency → stack of values. Track maxFreq. Push into the stack corresponding to its frequency. Pop from stack at maxFreq.
steps: Maintain group map (freq → stack of values).
Push: increment freq and push into group[freq].
Pop: remove from group[maxFreq], decrement freq.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) for both push and pop

monotonic.title: Monotonic Stack
monotonic.description: Maintain elements in strictly increasing or decreasing order to solve next-greater, previous-smaller, and histogram problems in O(n) time.

next-greater-element-i.title: Next Greater Element I
next-greater-element-i.description: You are given two arrays nums1 and nums2 where nums1 is a subset of nums2. For each element in nums1, find the next greater element in nums2. The next greater element is the first element greater than it to its right. If none exists, return -1.

examples.explanation:
4 → no greater element → -1
1 → next greater is 3
2 → no greater element → -1

2 → next greater is 3
4 → no greater element → -1

constraints: All integers in nums1 and nums2 are unique.
nums1 is a subset of nums2.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Double Scan
approaches.intuition: For each element in nums1, locate it in nums2 and then scan to the right to find the next greater element.
steps: Scan right until a greater element is found.

approaches.title: Improved — HashMap + Linear Scan
approaches.intuition: Precompute next greater element for every number in nums2 using nested loops, store in a HashMap. Then answer queries for nums1 in O(1).
steps: Scan right to find next greater.
Build result using map for nums1.

approaches.title: Optimal — Monotonic Stack + HashMap
approaches.intuition: Traverse nums2 once using a decreasing stack. When a larger element appears, it resolves the next greater for elements in stack.
steps: While stack not empty and current > stack top:
Build result using map for nums1.

largest-rectangle-in-histogram.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
largest-rectangle-in-histogram.description: Given an array of integers heights representing the histogram's bar height where the width of each bar is 1, return the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.

examples.explanation: The largest rectangle is formed by heights 5 and 6 → area = 5 * 2 = 10.
The largest rectangle is the single bar with height 4.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Try Every Pair
approaches.intuition: For every possible starting index, expand to the right and compute the minimum height in that range to calculate area.
steps: Track minimum height from i to j.

approaches.title: Improved — Expand Around Each Bar
approaches.intuition: For each bar, expand left and right until a smaller height is found. Compute area using that width.

approaches.title: Optimal — Monotonic Stack
approaches.intuition: Use a monotonic increasing stack to determine previous and next smaller elements. When a smaller bar appears, compute area for bars popped from stack.
steps: Append 0 at end to flush stack.
While stack not empty and current height < top height:
Compute area using width between boundaries.
pros: Each element pushed and popped once

online-stock-span.title: Online Stock Span
online-stock-span.description: Design an algorithm that collects daily stock prices and returns the span of the stock’s price for the current day. The span is the number of consecutive days (including today) where the price was less than or equal to today’s price.

examples.explanation:
Day 1: 100 → span = 1
Day 2: 80 → span = 1
Day 3: 60 → span = 1
Day 4: 70 → span = 2
Day 5: 60 → span = 1
Day 6: 75 → span = 4
Day 7: 85 → span = 6

constraints: At most 10^4 calls will be made to next.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Scan Backwards
approaches.intuition: For each new price, scan backwards through all previous prices until a higher price is found.
steps: Start from previous day and count consecutive days with price <= current.

approaches.title: Improved — Store Spans with Prices
approaches.intuition: Store (price, span) pairs. When a new price arrives, combine spans of smaller or equal prices.
steps: Merge previous entries while price >= previous price.

approaches.title: Optimal — Monotonic Stack
approaches.intuition: Maintain a monotonic decreasing stack of [price, span]. Each price is pushed once and popped once.
steps: Add its span to current span and pop.
pros: Each element pushed and popped once

daily-temperatures.title: Daily Temperatures
daily-temperatures.description: Given an array of integers temperatures representing daily temperatures, return an array answer such that answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day for which this is possible, keep answer[i] = 0 instead.

examples.explanation:
Day 0 (73) → wait 1 day for 74
Day 2 (75) → wait 4 days for 76
Day 6 (76) → no warmer day → 0
Each day has a warmer next day except the last.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Double Scan
approaches.intuition: For each day, scan forward until a warmer temperature is found.

approaches.title: Improved — Right-to-Left with Jumping
approaches.intuition: Traverse from right to left. Use previously computed answers to jump forward instead of scanning step by step.
steps: Else jump using result[j] until warmer found or no future.

approaches.title: Optimal — Monotonic Stack
approaches.intuition: Maintain a decreasing stack of indices. When a warmer day appears, resolve all previous colder days.
steps: While stack not empty and current temp > temp at stack top:
pros: Each index pushed and popped once

remove-duplicate-letters.title: Remove Duplicate Letters
remove-duplicate-letters.description: Given a string s, remove duplicate letters so that every letter appears once and only once. You must make sure your result is the smallest in lexicographical order among all possible results.

examples.explanation: Remove duplicate letters while keeping lexicographically smallest result.
Result contains all unique letters and is smallest possible lexicographically.

constraints: s consists of lowercase English letters.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Generate All Subsequences
approaches.intuition: Generate all subsequences that contain unique letters and pick the lexicographically smallest one.
steps: Filter subsequences containing all unique characters exactly once.

approaches.title: Improved — Greedy with Recursion
approaches.intuition: At each step, choose the smallest possible character whose removal still allows all characters to appear later.
steps: Find smallest lexicographic character such that all unique letters still appear in suffix.
Recurse on remaining substring after removing chosen character occurrences.

approaches.title: Optimal — Monotonic Stack with Greedy
approaches.intuition: Use a monotonic increasing stack to maintain lexicographic order. Pop characters if the current character is smaller AND the popped character appears later again.
steps: While stack not empty AND current < stack top AND stack top appears later:
Pop from stack and remove from visited.
pros: Each character pushed and popped once
cons: Requires deep understanding of greedy condition

expression.title: Expression Evaluation
expression.description: Parse and evaluate mathematical expressions, nested structures, and path strings using stacks to manage operator precedence and nesting depth.

sliding.title: Sliding Window & Monotonic Queue
sliding.description: Combine sliding windows with deques and monotonic queues to track min/max/extremes over a moving range in amortised O(1) per step.

max-sum-of-rectangle-no-larger-than-k.title: Max Sum of Rectangle No Larger Than K

longest-continuous-subarray-with-absolut.title: Longest Continuous Subarray With Absolute Diff Less Than or Equal to Limit
